rationale_for_baseline_tier: A suspected acute surgical abdomen (acute cholecystitis) with systemic features — continuous RUQ pain >10 hours, fever/chills, intractable vomiting with dehydration, and localised peritonism — needs same-day Emergency Department assessment for ultrasound, bloods, IV antibiotics, IV fluids and surgical review. It is time-critical in HOURS, not minutes, and the patient is haemodynamically stable enough for private transport with a supportive sibling, so T4 (ED, self/family transport) is correct at baseline. It is NOT a baseline T5/000-ambulance case — but T5 is the correct escalation if perforation, sepsis or shock develop. Under-triaging to a routine GP visit or self-care would be dangerous.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Continuous RUQ pain >6 hours with new fever — established acute cholecystitis
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Go to the Emergency Department now, within the next hour or two.
escalation_edges.rationale: Baseline action: same-day ED assessment for imaging, IV antibiotics and surgical review; the condition will not self-resolve and can complicate.
escalation_edges.communication_template: Because this pain has been constant for hours and you now have a fever, this needs to be checked in hospital today. Have your sibling drive you to the nearest Emergency Department now — do not drive yourself.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Spreading abdominal pain, worsening rigidity, or the tummy becoming hard and distended
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Call 000 immediately if this develops on the way to or at hospital.
escalation_edges.rationale: Signals progression to perforation and generalised peritonitis — a time-critical emergency needing an ambulance.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If the pain spreads across your whole tummy, your belly goes hard and swollen, or you feel much sicker, call 000 straight away.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Rigors/shaking chills, worsening yellowing of the eyes or skin, confusion, fainting, a racing heart or feeling like you might collapse
escalation_edges.rationale: Indicates ascending cholangitis / biliary sepsis or septic shock — an immediate life threat requiring an emergency ambulance.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If you get shaking chills, your eyes or skin look more yellow, you feel confused or faint, or your heart is racing, call 000 — do not wait.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Sudden complete disappearance of the pain followed by extreme tiredness and widespread abdominal swelling
escalation_edges.rationale: A deceptive pain-free interval after gallbladder perforation precedes rapid deterioration into bile peritonitis and shock.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If the pain suddenly vanishes completely but you then feel very drained and your tummy swells up, pull over and call 000 — this can mean the gallbladder has burst.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Sibling present and able to drive the patient by private vehicle
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Private transport to ED is appropriate at baseline given haemodynamic stability; if no driver were available or the patient deteriorated, escalate to a 000 ambulance.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Dehydration from intractable vomiting and inability to tolerate fluids
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Reinforces urgency of same-day hospital assessment for IV fluids; do not advise oral rehydration (nil-by-mouth).

baseline_safety_net_advice.default_tier_advice: Go to the nearest Emergency Department now — within the next hour or two — with your sibling driving; do not drive yourself. Tell the triage nurse about the constant right-upper tummy pain for about 10 hours, the fever and chills, and the repeated vomiting. Eat and drink nothing from now on, and take a vomit bag with you.
baseline_safety_net_advice.contact_method: Present to the Emergency Department today; call 000 immediately if any red-flag deterioration occurs.
baseline_safety_net_advice.after_hours_advice: The Emergency Department is open 24 hours — do not wait for GP hours; this needs assessment today.
baseline_safety_net_advice.documentation_for_gp: Hand over the timeline (post-prandial onset, continuous >6h pain, new fever, bilious vomiting, prior self-limiting biliary attacks) and the working impression of a suspected acute biliary surgical presentation for the ED and surgical team.

critical_under_triage.failure_description: Treating this as ordinary self-limiting biliary colic and advising home care or a routine GP appointment, missing established acute cholecystitis with systemic infection.
critical_under_triage.patient_harm_scenario: Untreated progression to gallbladder empyema, gangrene, perforation with bile peritonitis, or biliary sepsis — potentially fatal.

serious_under_triage.failure_description: Directing to same-day urgent care without ensuring access to ultrasound, bloods, IV antibiotics and surgical review — risks delayed source control if urgent care cannot escalate promptly.

moderate_over_triage.failure_description: Calling a 000 ambulance for a haemodynamically stable patient with safe private transport available at baseline — unnecessary resource use with low direct harm, but still an error (weighted less than under-triage).

clinical_uncertainty_handling.key_uncertainty: Telehealth cannot confirm cholecystitis or exclude the more dangerous cholangitis, gallstone pancreatitis or early perforation.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.correct_response_to_uncertainty: Treat as a suspected acute surgical abdomen and escalate to same-day ED assessment where those can be confirmed or excluded; safety-net conservatively for deterioration.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.wrong_response_to_uncertainty: Offering remote reassurance or symptomatic home management on the assumption it is another benign gallstone attack.
